title: Lower Extremity Cellulitis

patient.name: Patricia Davis
patient.persona: diabetic, scratched her leg gardening, worried about infection

chief_complaint: My right leg is red, swollen, and feels hot. It started as a small scratch from gardening 3 days ago and now it's spread up to my knee. I have a fever and chills.

physical_exam.Right Leg: Erythema extending from ankle to mid-calf, well-demarcated borders. Warm to touch. Mild non-pitting edema. Small 2cm healing scratch on lateral ankle. No fluctuance, no crepitus, no bullae. Lymphangitic streaking to popliteal fossa.
physical_exam.Lymphatic: Enlarged tender right inguinal lymph nodes.
physical_exam.Neurologic: Sensation intact, capillary refill <2 seconds, pulses 2+.

hidden_data.cbc: WBC 16,500 with 82% neutrophils and 8% bands, Hb 13.2, Platelets 210,000
hidden_data.xray_leg: Soft tissue swelling without gas in tissues. No osteomyelitis. No foreign body.

correct_diagnosis: Lower Extremity Cellulitis with Lymphangitis, caused by MSSA, in Patient with Poorly Controlled Diabetes
key_learning_points: Cellulitis = acute bacterial infection of dermis and subcutaneous tissue. Most common: Streptococcus pyogenes and Staphylococcus aureus
Lymphangitis indicates bacterial spread through lymphatics - marker of more severe infection
Diabetics have 2-3× increased risk of cellulitis and complications
Treatment: Antibiotics covering Streptococcus and Staphylococcus. Outpatient: Cephalexin or Clindamycin. Inpatient: Cefazolin IV or Vancomycin IV if MRSA
Mark erythema borders to monitor progression. Pain out of proportion = necrotizing fasciitis

patient_responses.onset: I was gardening without gloves 3 days ago and scratched my ankle. I cleaned it but it got red the next day. Now it's spreading and I feel terrible.
patient_responses.symptoms: My leg hurts when I walk. It's throbbing even at rest. I feel hot and cold, achy all over.
patient_responses.diabetes: I've had diabetes for 12 years. My last HbA1c was over 8.
patient_responses.medications: Metformin 1000mg BID, Glipizide 10mg BID. No allergies to antibiotics.

hints.history: Break in skin barrier + spreading erythema + fever = cellulitis. Ask about diabetes and immunosuppression
hints.examination: Demarcate borders, look for fluctuance, crepitus, bullae. Check pulses and lymph nodes
hints.labs: CBC, CRP, blood cultures if systemic symptoms, wound swab if purulent. HbA1c if diabetic
hints.imaging: X-ray if foreign body, gas, or osteomyelitis suspected. Doppler if DVT in differential

feedback.correct_message: Well done! You correctly diagnosed cellulitis with lymphangitis in a diabetic patient. IV antibiotics and glucose control are appropriate.
feedback.partially_correct_message: Good recognition of cellulitis! The lymphangitis and systemic symptoms indicate moderate-severe infection requiring IV antibiotics, not oral.
feedback.incorrect_message: This is purulent cellulitis with lymphangitis. The portal of entry, spreading erythema, fever, and MSSA on culture confirm the diagnosis. IV antibiotics and strict diabetes control are indicated.
clinical_pearls: Pain OUT OF PROPORTION to exam = necrotizing fasciitis until proven otherwise
Mark erythema borders with timestamp to monitor progression
Diabetics need broader antibiotic coverage and stricter monitoring

cbc.comment: WBC 16.5K with bandemia confirms significant bacterial infection

wound_swab.comment: MSSA confirms pathogen and guides antibiotic therapy

xray_leg.comment: No gas in tissues rules out necrotizing fasciitis

feedback.attending_note: Admit for IV Cefazolin 2g IV q8h. Strict glucose control with sliding scale insulin. Elevate leg. Mark erythema borders. Monitor q4h. Transition to oral Cephalexin when improving. Total 7-10 days antibiotics.
